pattern_recognition: Interval Merging, as the problem involves inserting a new interval into a sorted list of non-overlapping intervals and merging them if they overlap. Signals include the need to maintain order and handle overlaps efficiently.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Insert the new interval into the list and then iterate through the intervals to merge any overlapping ones.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Insert the new interval into the list. 2. Sort the list. 3. Iterate through the list and merge overlapping intervals.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize a single pass through the intervals to insert and merge the new interval without needing to sort the list again.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty result list. 2. Iterate through the existing intervals, adding them to the result until the new interval should be inserted. 3. Merge overlapping intervals with the new interval. 4. Add any remaining intervals.

dry_run: Given intervals: [(1, 3), (6, 9)] and new interval: (2, 5). Start with an empty result list. Add (1, 3) to the result. Since (2, 5) overlaps with (1, 3), merge them to form (1, 5). Skip to (6, 9) and add it to the result. Final result: [(1, 5), (6, 9)].
edge_cases: Empty input: [] should return []. Single element: [(1, 3)] with new (2, 5) should return [(1, 5)]. Duplicates: [(1, 3), (1, 3)] with new (2, 5) should return [(1, 5)]. Large input: handle efficiently without performance degradation. Overflow: ensure intervals do not exceed integer limits.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to merge intervals correctly can lead to missing overlaps. 2. Not maintaining the sorted order after insertion can cause incorrect results. 3. Failing to handle edge cases like fully overlapping intervals or intervals that touch at the edges.
